# Embedding Explorer {#embedding-explorer}

## What Embeddings Reveal

The Embedding Explorer lets you visualise the learned representations
of glycans and proteins as two-dimensional UMAP projections. These
embeddings capture structural and functional similarity: entities that
appear close together in the plot share biological characteristics,
even if they were never explicitly linked in the training data. By
projecting high-dimensional learned vectors into a plane you can see
at a glance how the model organises biological knowledge --- which
glycans it considers similar, which proteins it groups together, and
where the boundaries between functional categories fall.

The module supports two embedding spaces, and you switch between them
with a radio button at the top of the sidebar. Glycan embeddings come
from the knowledge-graph model (TransE or RotatE) with an original
dimensionality of 256, covering glycan structures drawn from
GlyTouCan and GlycoStore. Protein embeddings come from ESM-2, a
protein language model, at 1,280 dimensions, representing
glycosylation-related proteins such as enzymes and lectins. Both are
reduced to two dimensions with UMAP using `n_neighbors = 15` and
`min_dist = 0.1` before display. The UMAP parameters were chosen to
balance local neighbourhood fidelity against global structure
preservation, giving you a projection that is faithful at both the
cluster and landscape levels.

## Controlling the View

The sidebar offers several controls that shape what you see. The
"Colour-by" dropdown determines how points are painted. Choosing
"Cluster assignment" applies colours based on k-means cluster labels
computed during UMAP fitting, which is useful for identifying major
groupings without any prior biological annotation. Choosing "Glycan
type" (available only for glycan embeddings) colours points according
to the top-level glycan classification --- N-glycan, O-glycan, GAG,
and so on --- letting you see immediately whether the model's learned
structure aligns with established biochemical categories.

The "Highlight Specific IDs" text input accepts a comma-separated
list of identifiers. For glycans you might enter `G00051MO, G00065MO`;
for proteins, `Q10469, P15291`. Highlighted points are rendered with a
larger marker size and a contrasting black border so they stand out
clearly from the surrounding cloud. This is invaluable when you have a
specific glycan or protein in mind and want to locate it within the
broader embedding landscape.

```r
highlight_ids <- strsplit(input$highlight_ids, ",\\s*")[[1]]
umap_df$highlight <- umap_df$id %in% highlight_ids

plot_ly(umap_df, x = ~UMAP1, y = ~UMAP2,
        type = "scattergl", mode = "markers",
        marker = list(
          size = ifelse(umap_df$highlight, 12, 4),
          line = list(
            width = ifelse(umap_df$highlight, 2, 0),
            color = "black"
          )
        ))
```

A point-size slider (ranging from 1 to 10 pixels) lets you adjust the
base marker size. Smaller values work best when viewing the full
dataset, as they reduce overplotting and let cluster boundaries emerge
more clearly. Larger values help when you have zoomed into a sparse
region and individual points are hard to see. The label toggle
checkbox overlays text labels (glycan accessions or UniProt IDs) on
every point. It is off by default because labels create visual clutter
at full scale, but it becomes invaluable after you zoom into a region
of interest and want to identify specific entities.

## Reading the Glycan Embedding Space

The glycan UMAP reveals biologically meaningful structure that
validates the model's learning. High-mannose glycans (Man5 through
Man9) form a tight, isolated cluster, reflecting their conserved
biosynthetic origin --- they all share the same core and differ only
in the number of mannose residues, so the model correctly represents
them as highly similar. Complex-type glycans spread over a much larger
area, subdivided by antenna count and terminal modifications such as
sialylation and fucosylation. This dispersion makes biological sense:
complex glycans encompass enormous structural diversity, and the model
captures that by distributing them across a wide region of embedding
space.

Hybrid-type glycans sit between the high-mannose and complex clusters,
consistent with their intermediate processing stage in the
biosynthetic pathway. They share structural features with both
groups --- the mannose-rich arm of a hybrid glycan resembles
high-mannose structures, while its processed arm resembles complex
ones --- and the UMAP faithfully reflects this dual nature.

## Reading the Protein Embedding Space

The protein embedding space tells a complementary story. Proteins with
similar glycosylation profiles --- those that act on overlapping
substrate sets --- appear proximal to one another, indicating that
ESM-2's sequence representations encode functionally relevant
information beyond mere sequence identity. Sialyltransferases (the
ST3GAL and ST6GAL families) cluster together, well separated from
mannosidases, reflecting their distinct enzymatic activities. Because
ESM-2 captures sequence-level similarity, isoenzymes such as FUT1 and
FUT2 end up close together even when their substrate specificity
differs. This is a useful reminder that embedding proximity reflects
sequence and structural similarity, which correlates with but does not
perfectly predict functional identity.

Comparing the glycan UMAP clusters with the hierarchy levels in
Chapter \@ref(hierarchy-browser) reveals how structural containment
maps onto embedding proximity. Glycans that are close in the hierarchy
tend to be close in embedding space, but the embedding also captures
cross-branch similarities that the hierarchy cannot express.

## Demo Mode and Generating Real Data

If UMAP coordinates have not been pre-computed, the module
automatically enters demo mode. A banner at the top reads "Showing 500
random demo points. Run the preparation script for real data." In this
mode, 500 random points sampled from a multivariate normal
distribution illustrate the interface. All controls --- colour-by,
highlight, labels --- remain fully functional, so you can familiarise
yourself with the interface and practice the workflow before loading
real data.

To replace the demo with actual embeddings, run the preparation script
from the repository root:

```bash
cd /home/matsui/github/glycoMusubi
python3 prepare_shiny_data.py --task umap
```

This script reads raw embeddings from `outputs/embeddings/`, computes
UMAP projections, and writes the results to
`shiny_app/data/umap_glycan.parquet` and
`shiny_app/data/umap_protein.parquet`. It requires `umap-learn`
(version 0.5.3 or later), `torch` (2.0 or later), `numpy` (1.24 or
later), and `pandas` (2.0 or later). Typical runtime is two to five
minutes on a machine with 32 GB of RAM for roughly 58,000 glycan
points and 12,000 protein points. You can verify that the output files
were created successfully with:

```bash
ls -lh shiny_app/data/umap_*.parquet
```

## Performance Considerations

The plot uses plotly's WebGL renderer (`type = "scattergl"`) rather
than the default SVG renderer, and this choice matters enormously at
scale. With 10,000 points both renderers feel smooth, but at 50,000
points the SVG renderer becomes sluggish while WebGL stays responsive.
At 100,000 points SVG is effectively unusable --- the browser
struggles to manage that many DOM elements --- whereas WebGL remains
usable with only minor lag because it offloads rendering to the GPU.

If you experience slow rendering on an older machine or one without a
dedicated GPU, the simplest remedy is to filter the display to a
single glycan type or cluster. This reduces the point count
dramatically and restores the snappy interaction you need for
productive exploration.

## Exploring Glycan Clusters Step by Step

Open the Embedding Explorer tab and select "Glycan" as the embedding
type. Set the colour-by control to "Glycan type" to see the major
categories painted across the plot. Zoom into the high-mannose cluster
(usually found in the top-left region, though UMAP layouts are
stochastic so the exact position may vary) and enable labels to read
individual GlyTouCan accessions. If a particular glycan catches your
eye, paste its ID into the highlight box and press Enter to make it
visually prominent. Then switch to "Protein" embeddings and colour by
cluster to see how enzymes group by function.

For more advanced exploration, use plotly's lasso tool to draw a
freehand region around a group of points and inspect the selected
entities in the table below the plot. Double-clicking the plot area
resets to the original view. You can note the cluster ID of an
interesting glycan group and then cross-reference its prediction
accuracy in the Benchmark Dashboard (Chapter \@ref(benchmarks)). The
plotly camera icon in the toolbar exports a publication-quality PNG at
the current zoom level, suitable for inclusion in papers or
presentations.
